message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:label>(1)</jats:label>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Here, we characterize COVID-19 2
                    <jats:sup>nd</jats:sup>
                    waves, following a study presenting negative associations between 1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    wave COVID-19 spread parameters and temperature;
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:label>(2)</jats:label>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Visual examinations of daily increase in confirmed COVID-19 cases in 124 countries, determined 1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    and 2
                    <jats:sup>nd</jats:sup>
                    waves in 28 countries;
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:label>(3)</jats:label>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    wave spread rate increases with country mean elevation, temperature, time since wave onset, and median age. Spread rates decrease above 1000m, indicating high UV decrease spread rate. For 2nd waves, associations are opposite: viruses adapted to high temperature and to infect young populations. Earliest 2
                    <jats:sup>nd</jats:sup>
                    waves started April 5-7 at mutagenic high elevations (Armenia, Algeria). 2
                    <jats:sup>nd</jats:sup>
                    waves occurred also at warm-to-cold season transition (Argentina, Chile). Spread decreases in most (77%) countries. Death-to-total case ratios decrease during the 2
                    <jats:sup>nd</jats:sup>
                    wave, also when comparing with the same period for countries where the 1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    wave is ongoing. In countries with late 1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    wave onset, spread rates fit better 2
                    <jats:sup>nd</jats:sup>
                    than 1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    wave-temperature patterns; In countries with ageing populations (examples: Japan, Sweden, Ukraine), 2
                    <jats:sup>nd</jats:sup>
                    waves only adapted to spread at higher temperatures, not to infect children.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:label>(4)</jats:label>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    wave viruses evolved towards lower spread and mortality. 2
                    <jats:sup>nd</jats:sup>
                    wave mutant COVID-19 strain(s) adapted to higher temperature, infecting children and replace (also in cold conditions) 1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    wave COVID-19 strains. Counterintuitively, low spread strains replace high spread strains, rendering prognostics and extrapolations uncertain.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title:
Inverted covariate effects for mutated 2
                  <sup>nd</sup>
                  vs 1
                  <sup>st</sup>
                  wave Covid-19: high temperature spread biased for young

reference.article-title: Temperature decreases spread parameters of the new covid-19 cases dynamics

reference.unstructured: Yao, H. , Lu, X. , Chen, Q. , Xu, K. , Chen, Y. , Cheng, L. , Liu, F. , Wu, Z. , Wu, H. , Jin, C. , Zheng, M. , Wu, N. , Jiang, C. , Li, L. Patient-derived mutations impact pathogenicity of SARS-CoV-2. MedRxiv preprint 2020, doi:https://doi.org/10.1101/2020.04.14.20060160.
Wikipedia. Available online: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_ population_ density (accessed on 6 June 2020).
Demongeot, J. , Seligmann, H. Covid-19 and miRNA-like inhibition power. Biology (Basel) 2020, submitted.

reference.article-title: Understanding Unreported Cases in the COVID-19 Epidemic Outbreak in Wuhan, China, and the Importance of Major Public Health Interventions
